# The Weaver of Night

Long ago, when the sky was empty and black, the world knew only the harsh light of day and the absolute darkness of night. People feared the night, for in its void lurked unknown terrors.

A young girl named Lyra loved her grandmother, a skilled weaver who created tapestries of breathtaking beauty. When her grandmother passed away, Lyra wept for seven days and seven nights. On the eighth night, she gathered all her grandmother's silver threads and climbed the highest mountain.

"Grandmother taught me that beauty drives away fear," Lyra whispered to the wind. She began to weave, casting the silver threads into the endless black canvas above. With each throw, a thread caught and held, shimmering with captured moonlight.

She wove all through the night—here a cluster of light, there a trail across the darkness. Some threads she pulled tight into brilliant points, others she let hang loose and twinkling. By dawn, the sky was adorned with thousands of silver lights.

The gods, moved by her love and artistry, made her creation eternal. Now, whenever night falls, Lyra's tapestry unfolds across the heavens, and people no longer fear the dark—they marvel at the stars instead.

# The First Singer

In the time before time, the Sky was empty and dark, and the Earth below had no light to see by. The Great Weaver sat alone at her loom, creating the fabric of existence thread by thread, but she worked in darkness and grew weary.

One day, a small bird flew up from the Earth, having felt its way through the blackness. "Why do you work without light?" the bird asked.

"There is no light to work by," the Great Weaver replied sadly.

The bird began to sing—a song so pure and beautiful that the Great Weaver wept. Each tear that fell from her eyes turned to silver light as it rose into the Sky, becoming a star. The more the bird sang, the more tears of joy fell upward, until the entire Sky blazed with countless stars.

The Great Weaver rewarded the bird by giving it the most beautiful voice of all creatures. "Sing each dawn," she told the bird, "and chase away the darkness, so all may remember that beauty creates light."

This is why birds still sing at sunrise, calling back the light, their ancient duty to awaken the world.

Myth: In the beginning, silence covered everything like a heavy blanket, and the Great Weaver worked alone in the void. She created mountains and rivers, forests and plains, but they existed in perfect, terrible silence.

One morning, the smallest of all her creations—a sparrow—opened its beak and released the first sound ever heard. The note was fragile and uncertain, but it shattered the silence like breaking glass.

The Great Weaver stopped her work, amazed. "Again," she whispered.

The sparrow sang louder, and where its song touched the darkness above, cracks appeared. Through each crack poured golden light, creating the first sunrise. The bird sang on, and more cracks spread across the sky—these became the stars, forever leaking light into the world.

But the effort exhausted the little sparrow. As it fell, the Great Weaver caught it gently. "You have given the world its voice," she said. "In return, I give you companions."

She breathed life into a thousand birds, each with their own song. "Share this gift," she told them. "Let every dawn begin with singing, so silence never returns."

And so it remains.
